summary: Martin Fowler describes his text-based book writing toolchain, developed over two decades of technical authorship. He moved from WYSIWYG tools like Framemaker to a custom XML-based system that uses semantic markup and transforms to Docbook for multiple output formats. The approach enables automatic code import from live programs, eliminating error-prone copy/paste. While non-geeks might find it primitive, the system integrates well with version control and collaborative workflows, including his publisher's production staff working directly in the repository.
key_points: Text-based source formats enable version control and programmatic processing that WYSIWYG tools cannot match
Semantic markup (like <term> instead of <bold>) separates meaning from presentation and enables richer transformations like automatic indexing
Automatic code import from live, tested programs eliminates the error-prone copy/paste step that plagued his earlier books
Docbook serves as an excellent transformation target even if it's too verbose as a source format, providing paths to HTML, PDF, ePub, and print
The entire toolchain treats writing like software development: text files, version control, automated builds, and collaborative workflows
money_quotes: I'm a geek after all, and it's easy for me to write tools to process text-based files.
Now with my current scheme, all code is automatically imported into the book as part of the build process. So if I update my code, the book automatically becomes up to date.
I prefer this because it forces me to focus on semantics rather than formatting.
I prefer XML because it gives me more flexibility in markup - I can introduce whatever tags I like, including specialist tags for just this book.
While I don't like Docbook as a source document format, it is really good as a transformation target.
Like any programmer I value version control highly.
key_insight: Treating book writing like software development—with text-based source files, semantic markup, automated builds, and version control—solves the unique challenges technical authors face with code examples and collaboration.
